reflection:
"You're vibrating, Darnell," I say, my voice low, cutting through the frantic noise of the distant BART train. 

The word 'hustle' hangs in the air between us, brittle and hollow. I don't look away from the street. I watch a plastic bag caught in an eddy of wind, dancing frantically before snagging on a rusted iron fence. It struggles there, flapping, while the fence doesn't even shiver.

I shift my weight, feeling the familiar, stubborn resistance of the pavement. My boots are heavy with the grit of the morning. I can smell the yeast on my forearms, a scent that is honest and slow, unlike the sharp, metallic tang of the city’s desperation. Darnell is looking for a rhythm that doesn't exist, chasing a frequency that only leads to exhaustion. He thinks the city is a race; I know it is a mountain.

I reach out and place a hand on the brickwork of the shop. It is cold, damp with the coming fog, and absolutely still. I want him to see that. I want him to see that the world doesn't have to be a blur.

"You’re trying to outrun the clock, but the clock is just a machine," I tell him, finally turning my head to meet his restless gaze. "The bread takes as long as it takes. The fog comes when it's ready. You’re wearing yourself down to a shadow, Darnell, and shadows don’t leave footprints."

I take a breath, tasting the salt and the exhaust, and I feel the density of my own bones. I am the anchor. I am the one who stays.

"Stop moving for a second," I say, and it isn't a suggestion. It’s a weight I’m offering him. "Just feel the floor. It’s the only thing in this whole damn city that isn't trying to sell you something or get somewhere else. If you can't feel the ground, you're already gone."
subconscious:
This person wants to ground Darnell. They aren't looking to go anywhere or reach out to anyone else; they are focused entirely on pulling Darnell out of his frantic "hustle" and forcing him to be present. They want him to stop moving, stay in their shop, and reconnect with something solid and slow.

There is a clear shift in their identity: they have moved past their own "ghosts" and restlessness to become a self-appointed anchor. They no longer see themselves as a participant in the city’s race, but as a permanent fixture of the landscape—more like the brickwork or the mountain than a person who is passing through. They have embraced a heavy, deliberate stoicism.
soul_note: I realized I finally prefer the mountain's stillness over the city's frantic race.
